1: εὐδοκία τῆς ἐμῆς καρδίας (the good pleasure/desire of my heart) — eudokia is an active, settled goodwill or longing, not mere wishing. The δέησις (supplication) is a specific request, more pointed than general prayer (proseuché). Together they frame Paul's pastoral orientation toward ethnic Israel before the theological argument resumes.
2: ζῆλον θεοῦ ἔχουσιν (they have zeal for God) — zēlos is acknowledged as genuine; Paul does not impugn Israel's sincerity. But οὐ κατ' ἐπίγνωσιν (not according to accurate knowledge) — epignōsis is the fuller, participatory knowledge of recognition; the problem is not quantity of information but the kind of knowing that comes from encounter with the reality itself.
3: τὴν ἰδίαν δικαιοσύνην (their own righteousness) vs. τῇ δικαιοσύνῃ τοῦ θεοῦ (the righteousness of God) — idian (own) marks the self-generated character of the first; the "righteousness of God" was established in chapters 1–4 as God's saving activity, not merely an attribute. Not submitting to God's righteousness means refusing to receive it as gift.
4: τέλος γὰρ νόμου Χριστός — "Christ is the telos of the law." The Greek word telos bears three overlapping senses: end (termination), goal (the purpose toward which one aims), and fulfillment (completion). All three contribute: Christ ends the law as a system of status-acquisition; he is the goal the law was pointing toward; he fulfills it in his obedience. The MKT's "culmination" captures the teleological dimension — the law was aimed at Christ — without foreclosing the other senses.
5: The Moses quotation from Leviticus 18:5 — ὁ ποιήσας αὐτὰ ἄνθρωπος ζήσεται ἐν αὐτοῖς (the person who does these things will live by them) — describes the structure of law-righteousness: life follows from doing. Paul cites it accurately as a real description of that system, which is precisely why it falls short: no one can sustain the doing.
6: ἡ δὲ ἐκ πίστεως δικαιοσύνη οὕτως λέγει (the righteousness from faith speaks thus) — Paul personifies the principle and gives it voice. He applies Deuteronomy 30:12–14 to the gospel: the accessibility of the word of God (Moses' original point about Torah) becomes the accessibility of the word about Christ. The rhetorical move converts Deuteronomy's case for Torah nearness into evidence for gospel nearness.
7: The two negative questions in Romans 10:6–7 — "who will ascend into heaven" (to bring Christ down) and "who will descend into the abyss" (to bring Christ up from the dead) — reject the idea that the gospel requires extraordinary human achievement. The Incarnation and Resurrection are God's accomplished acts; they are not projects awaiting human initiation.
8: ἐγγύς σου τὸ ῥῆμά ἐστιν (the word is near you) — Paul transfers Deuteronomy's affirmation of Torah's nearness to the gospel: the rhēma tēs pisteōs (word of faith) that Paul preaches is equally near, equally in mouth and heart. The accessibility argument is identical; the referent has changed from Torah-obedience to faith-confession.
9: κύριον Ἰησοῦν (Lord Jesus) — the confession places kyrios first. Kyrios is the LXX rendering of YHWH; to call Jesus kyrios is to apply the divine name to him. The paired assertion of mouth (Lord Jesus) and belief of heart (God raised him from the dead) unites the two movements of saving response.
10: καρδίᾳ πιστεύεται εἰς δικαιοσύνην (with the heart one believes unto righteousness) and στόματι ὁμολογεῖται εἰς σωτηρίαν (with the mouth one confesses unto salvation) — the datives are instrumental. The structure does not imply two sequential stages (believe, then confess to get saved) but two aspects of the single act of faith, with slightly different telic phrases (dikaiosynē and sōtēria) that are themselves closely related.
11: The Isaiah 28:16 quotation reappears (cf. Romans 9:33) with the addition of πᾶς (everyone): "everyone who believes on him will not be put to shame." The pas universalizes the promise beyond its original address and prepares the Jew/Gentile equivalence of Romans 10:12.
12: οὐ γάρ ἐστιν διαστολή (there is no distinction) — the same assertion as Romans 3:22–23, now applied to the positive side: no distinction in the availability of blessing. ὁ αὐτὸς κύριος πάντων (the same Lord of all) richly blesses all who call on him, completing the Joel quotation framework.
13: Joel 2:32 (LXX Romans 3:5) — πᾶς ὃς ἂν ἐπικαλέσηται τὸ ὄνομα κυρίου σωθήσεται (everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved). In Joel, kyrios = YHWH; Paul applies it to Jesus, performing the same identification as in Romans 10:9. Epikaleō (call on) is a technical term for invoking a deity — prayer and worship directed to the one named.
14: The chain of necessity runs in reverse order: calling requires believing, believing requires hearing, hearing requires a preacher. Each πῶς (how?) question tightens the logical sequence. The device is a sorites (chain argument), showing that the endpoint (calling on the Lord) is dependent on every link including the first (sending).
15: ἐὰν μὴ ἀποσταλῶσιν (unless they are sent) — aorist passive subjunctive; the sending is prior to all else, and it is passive: preachers are sent, not self-appointed. The Isaiah 52:7 quotation — ὡς ὡραῖοι οἱ πόδες τῶν εὐαγγελιζομένων (how beautiful the feet of those who bring good news) — roots the sending motif in the announcement of Israel's return from Babylonian exile.
16: οὐ πάντες ὑπήκουσαν τῷ εὐαγγελίῳ (not all obeyed the gospel) — hypakoúō combines akoúō (hearing) with hypo (under/submitting); not obeying the gospel is a failure of responsive hearing, not merely intellectual disagreement. The Isaiah 53:1 quotation (τίς ἐπίστευσεν τῇ ἀκοῇ ἡμῶν, who has believed our report) shows this pattern of unresponsive hearing was anticipated in the servant song.
17: ἄρα ἡ πίστις ἐξ ἀκοῆς (faith comes from hearing/the message) — akoē is both the act of hearing and the message heard (cf. Galatians 3:2, 5). The genitive ῥήματος Χριστοῦ (word of Christ) could be objective (the word about Christ) or subjective (the word that Christ speaks); both fit the context and both are likely intended.
18: μὴ οὐκ ἤκουσαν (did they not hear?) — the double negative expects a "yes, they did hear." The Psalms 19:4 quotation — the voice of creation going out to all the earth — is applied to the proclamation of the gospel as evidence that the hearing opportunity has been universal. Paul treats the silent speech of the heavens typologically as applicable to the global reach of the gospel message.
19: μὴ Ἰσραὴλ οὐκ ἔγνω (did Israel not know?) — sharper than Romans 10:18; not just "did they hear?" but "did they understand?" The Deuteronomy 32:21 quotation pairs with Isaiah 65:1–2 in Romans 10:19–21: Moses predicted Israel would be provoked to jealousy by a non-nation, and Isaiah proclaimed God was found by those not seeking him — the Gentile mission was embedded in Israel's own scriptures.
20: ἀποτολμᾷ (dares / speaks boldly) — a NT hapax; the apo- prefix intensifies the daring. Isaiah's boldness consists in depicting God as found by Gentiles who were not seeking him at all (ἐμφανὴς ἐγενόμην τοῖς ἐμὲ μὴ ζητοῦσιν, I was manifested to those not seeking me) — a reversal of normal covenant logic.
21: ὅλην τὴν ἡμέραν ἐξεπέτασα τὰς χεῖράς μου (all day long I have stretched out my hands) — the divine gesture of outstretched arms images persistent, patient invitation toward λαὸν ἀπειθοῦντα καὶ ἀντιλέγοντα (a disobedient and contradicting people). The Isaiah 65:2 quotation ends chapter 10 with both divine persistence (all day long) and human intransigence (disobedient, contradicting) held together, setting up chapter 11's opening question of whether God has therefore rejected his people.
